result:
The first time I ever tasted a mango I was eight and sitting on the back steps of a house that wasn’t mine. The steps were painted a peeling robin’s-egg blue, and the mango had traveled farther than I had—Ecuador, the sticker said—while I had only ever crossed the river from New Jersey to Philadelphia to visit cousins. My mother handed me the fruit like it was homework: “Figure it out.” She didn’t mean the mango; she meant the world. I bit straight through the skin, fuzzy as a peach but tougher, and the stringy flesh resisted my incisors so that I came away with fibers wedged between my teeth and juice running down my chin like neon paint. I thought I had broken something important inside my mouth, but it was only sugar teaching me the difference between wound and sweetness. That night I wrote “mango” on the inside of my forearm with a leaking pen so I wouldn’t forget the word for the shock of foreign sunlight. The ink sank into the pale map of veins and stayed for a week, a temporary tattoo spelling want.

Years later, in a hostel kitchen in Lisbon, I met a woman who carried a pocketknife sharp enough to shave the centuries off stone. She spoke Danish to the tomatoes while she slivered them, English to the onions to keep them from weeping, and Portuguese to the sea salt because, she said, salt remembers the language of the last person who truly respected it. When I told her the mango story she laughed once—an abrupt sound like a chair leg scraping tile—and then she cut me a slice of tomato so thin I could read my future through it. I saw nothing, but I ate it anyway, and the seeds lodged in my molars like encrypted confessions. We didn’t exchange names. Instead she taught me how to fold a paper napkin into a sailboat, set it adrift on the surface of my soup, and make a wish as it dissolved. I wished for more time, but I didn’t say so; I just watched the bread soak up the wish until the broth looked defeated. She left before sunrise. The only proof I hadn’t invented her is a scar on my left thumb shaped like the crescent moon over Alfama, acquired while washing her knife in the dark.

I keep a list of almost-cities the way some people collect stamps or ghosts: Valparaíso, where I arrived during a bus-driver strike and had to coast downhill with strangers pushing the coach like a drunken boulder; Reykjavík in February when the streetlights flickered Morse code at the northern lights and I forgot to sleep for three days; Tbilisi at dawn during a wine harvest, when an old woman pressed a still-warm loaf into my hands and I understood bread could be a passport if you tore it right. Each place gave me a small, unlabelled object—green copper coin, lava rock wrapped in Icelandic wool, splinter of vine wood—and I carry them in a tin once meant for breath mints. Whenever the plane tilts for descent I shake the tin like maracas, a pocket-sized earthquake to remind myself I have weight somewhere.

But the truth is I am always half in and half out, a perpetual passenger in the copper-colored dusk that resides between time zones. My friends have learned to recognize the lag in my voice before I do: syllables arriving late, like suitcases that took the wrong carousel. They ask where I’m going next and I answer with fragments because whole sentences feel arrogant. “North,” I say, or “toward the smell of diesel and jasmine.” I used to think constant motion would erode the grief I wore like an undergarment no one could see, but motion only rearranges the pattern of the weave. Some nights the fabric tightens across my collarbones and I wake up tasting aluminum; other nights it slackens and I dream of a house with a porch swing that nobody is pushing. In the dream I sit very still, listening for trains that never come, and I feel something close to mercy.

Once, on a night ferry across the Sea of Marmara, a boy of maybe seven stood beside me at the railing and recited the planets in Turkish. When he got to Dünya—Earth—he pointed at my chest, not his own, as if the planet were portable and I had volunteered to carry it. Then he ran off, barefoot on the metal deck, and I realized I had never considered ownership in reverse: not that I belong to any patch of soil, but that the entire blue-green whirl might belong to me, to all of us, the way a library book belongs to each borrower while it waits to be returned. I pressed my passport against the rail and felt the ferry’s engine drumming through the laminate like a second heartbeat trying to sync with mine. For the rest of the crossing I whispered “Dünya” every time the waves slapped the hull, a lullaby for a spherical infant that cries tectonically.

I wonder what becomes of the languages we almost learn. I can still count to five in Vietnamese thanks to a street vendor in Da Nang who traded numbers for mangosteens, but I never learned six, so my arithmetic falls off a cliff. I can say “thank you” in sixteen tongues, “sorry” in twelve, and “I love you” in one and a half—English collapses under that sentence, requires scaffolding, whereas in Portuguese the phrase is lighter, ear-shaped, but I pronounce it like someone gargling marbles, so only half counts. Sometimes I imagine all the abandoned syllables orbiting overhead, a debris field of gratitude, apology, longing, clinking against satellites. Astronauts probably hear them at night: tiny phonetic meteorites pinging the hull, begging reentry.

The paradox, of course, is that I write this from a borrowed desk facing a cornfield that doesn’t care whether I stay. The wind combs the tassels like a practiced babysitter. I have lived in this house for three months—longer than anywhere since college—and I keep waiting for the walls to accuse me of betrayal. Instead they inhale and exhale like lung tissue, indifferent. Every morning I walk the dirt road until the gravel turns to dust and the dust to a view of cell towers pretending to be pines. I bring back artifacts: broken baling twine, bird skull the color of antique piano keys, receipt for diesel someone lost. I arrange them on the windowsill the way my grandmother once lined up spice jars in alphabetical order—an incantation against chaos. At dusk swallows stitch the sky and I feel the hours buttoned to my skin like borrowed coat toggles. When I leave, I will unzip them silently and let the next occupant find the warmth still inside.

People ask what I’m searching for and I respond with the only honest thing: the moment before the mango, before I knew sweetness could be violent, when possibility tasted only of stroller handles and summer rain on asphalt. Yet I don’t want to return; I want to keep approaching that instant laterally, like a ship tacking against wind, close enough to salute but never anchoring. The Dane with the knife understood: some wishes must be confessed to broth, not granted. So I keep moving, not to escape but to stay in conversation with departure itself, the way violinists speak to silence by drawing a bow across it. Every border is a syllable in a run-on sentence I am composing in midair, and when I finally exhaust my vocabulary—when the tin of coins and rocks rattles empty—I will stand still, open my mouth, and let the wind finish the story.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
